findings.description: Unsanitized user input concatenated directly into SQL query via username and password parameters. Attackers can inject arbitrary SQL to bypass authentication or exfiltrate data.
findings.remediation: Use PreparedStatement with parameterized queries: String query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE username=? AND password=?'; pstmt.setString(1, username); pstmt.setString(2, password);

findings.description: String concatenation used in SQL query with user-controlled keyword parameter. Even with PreparedStatement, the query string is constructed dynamically.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized LIKE queries: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM products WHERE name LIKE ?', (f'%{keyword}%',))

findings.description: User-controlled 'comment' parameter is output directly to HTML response without encoding, enabling reflected XSS attacks.
findings.remediation: Use OWASP Java Encoder or similar: out.println(Encode.forHtml(comment))

findings.description: Database password 'SuperSecret123!@#' is hardcoded in source code, exposed in version control and accessible to anyone with code access.
findings.remediation: Use environment variables or secure vault: String DB_PASSWORD = System.getenv('DB_PASSWORD');

findings.description: User-controlled filename parameter concatenated into file path without validation. Attackers can use '../' sequences to access files outside /app/uploads/.
findings.remediation: Validate and sanitize filename: Path base = Paths.get('/app/uploads').toAbsolutePath().normalize(); Path resolved = base.resolve(filename).normalize(); if (!resolved.startsWith(base)) throw new SecurityException();

findings.description: User-controlled 'host' parameter concatenated into OS command executed via Runtime.exec. Attackers can inject command separators to execute arbitrary commands.
findings.remediation: Use ProcessBuilder with argument list instead: new ProcessBuilder('ping', '-c', '4', host).start();

summary: Code uses PreparedStatement with parameterized queries - no SQL injection vulnerability detected.
Code properly encodes user input with OWASP Encoder before HTML output - no XSS vulnerability detected.

findings.description: strcpy copies user_input into 64-byte buffer without bounds checking. Input longer than 64 bytes will overflow the stack buffer.
findings.remediation: Use strncpy with size limit: strncpy(buffer, user_input, sizeof(buffer)-1); buffer[sizeof(buffer)-1] = '\0';

findings.description: User-controlled string passed directly as format string to printf. Format specifiers like %s, %n, %x can read/write arbitrary memory.
findings.remediation: Use printf with format specifier: printf('%s', user_msg);

findings.description: f-string used to construct SQL query with user-controlled 'username' parameter. Enables arbitrary SQL injection.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ?', (username,))

findings.description: os.system called with f-string containing user-controlled 'host' parameter. Shell metacharacters can inject arbitrary commands.
findings.remediation: Use subprocess with list: subprocess.run(['ping', '-c', '4', host], capture_output=True)

findings.description: Payment API key hardcoded in class variable. This sensitive credential is exposed in source control.
User-controlled URL passed directly to requests.get(). Attackers can supply internal URLs (localhost, 169.254.169.254 for metadata) to access internal services.
findings.remediation: Validate URL against allowlist and block internal IP ranges. Use urllib.parse to check scheme and netloc.

summary: Parameterized query properly used with sqlite3 - no vulnerability.
subprocess.run used with argument list - no shell injection vulnerability.
